narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 39, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 65% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on pushing the prediction toward failure or success. The strongest driver was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, which significantly raised concerns about your trajectory heading into law school and the bar. Next, your LSAT score of 39—stronger than the average of 36.7—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you'd pass by showing solid analytical promise. Finally, your Year 1 decile of 3, lower than the average around 5.7, added some upward pressure toward the failure prediction, hinting at early academic hurdles that lingered. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later deciles stand out as weaker, while your LSAT and GPA (above the 3.21 average) align more favorably, but not enough to offset the rest. Full-time status, like most applicants at 93%, was neutral here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes bar passage stats schools track closely. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening recent academics through post-bac courses or targeted prep to lift those decile concerns. Reapply after building a clearer upward trend, perhaps with letters highlighting your resilience and community ties. You've got strengths to leverage; persistence pays off for students like you.
